mnemonics: SLLC: Static Like a Line, Linked Like a Chain. (A waiting line is a fixed, static structure, while a chain's links are dynamically connected and can be rearranged.)
keyPoints: Static allocation uses a single, contiguous block of memory (like an array), set at compile time.
Linked allocation uses non-contiguous memory blocks (nodes) connected by pointers, allowing dynamic growth/shrinkage.
Insertion/deletion in a static structure often requires shifting elements, while a linked structure only requires pointer updates.
commonMistakes: Assuming linked allocation is always faster; for random access by index, static (array) is O(1) while linked is O(n).
Forgetting to manage memory manually in linked allocation, leading to memory leaks (in languages without garbage collection).
